scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: AI in your classroom. [pause] You've been using it for years. [pause] You just didn't call it that.

scenes.name: Already here
scenes.text: Spell-check. Translation. Video recommendations. Adaptive practice apps. [pause] AI has been quietly in your school for over a decade. [pause] What changed recently — it became <emphasis>visible</emphasis>.

scenes.name: Adaptive
scenes.text: <emphasis>Adaptive learning</emphasis> is the oldest form. [pause] Platforms that build a model of each student and adjust difficulty in real time. [pause] Khan Academy, Duolingo, national platforms. All AI underneath.

scenes.name: Assessment
scenes.text: <emphasis>Automated assessment</emphasis> grades multiple choice, flags plagiarism, spots patterns. [pause] Useful for routine. [pause] But it cannot judge creativity, originality, or the quality of thinking. [pause] Only patterns.

scenes.name: Content shift
scenes.text: What's <emphasis>new</emphasis> — content creation. [pause] Large language models can draft lesson plans, differentiate texts, write quiz questions, translate, brainstorm. [pause] One tool. Many tasks. [pause] That's the real shift.

scenes.name: Skills transfer
scenes.text: Your existing skills transfer directly. [pause] Writing learning objectives equals writing prompts. Differentiating equals asking for variations. Evaluating student work equals evaluating AI output. Scaffolding tasks equals breaking down complex requests.

scenes.name: Changes
scenes.text: What's <emphasis>changing:</emphasis> speed of preparation, range of tasks AI can help with, expectations on schools. [pause] What's <emphasis>not</emphasis> changing: the teacher-student relationship, the need for professional judgment, the fact that learning is social.

scenes.name: Start small
scenes.text: Where to begin. [pause] Pick one routine task — quiz questions, a parent letter, a worksheet. [pause] Try AI for it. Compare to your own work. [pause] Talk to colleagues. [pause] Be patient — it's a new professional skill.
